question: How can we access private method of a class from outside the class?
answer: We can use Reflection to access private method of a class from outside the class. IN Java, we use getDeclaredMethod() to get instance of a private method. Then we mark this method accessible and finally invoke it. In following sample code, we are accessing private method message() of class Foo by Reflection. FileName: Foo.java public class Foo { private void message(){System.out.println("hello java"); } } FileName: FooMethodCall.java import java.lang.reflect.Method; public class FooMethodCall{ public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{ Class c = Class.forName("Foo"); Object o= c.newInstance(); Method m =c.getDeclaredMethod("message", null); m.setAccessible(true); m.invoke(o, null); } }

question: Why do we use Static Nested interface in Java?
answer: Only the enclosing class can access a Static Nested interface. Consider following code in which interface Xyz is enclosed in class Abc. public class Abc { public interface Xyz { void callback(); } public static void registerCallback(Xyz xyz) {...} } // Client Code Abc.registerCallback(new Abc.Xyz() { public void callback() {...} }); Any code that cannot access Abc can not access interface Xyz also. So the purpose of declaring an Inner interface is to restrict its access from outside world. String
